steps.description: Install the toolchain's dependencies (TypeScript; esbuild and puppeteer-core for builds and visual tests). The clone ships prebuilt — no build step before first run.
Start the dev server on http://127.0.0.1:8200/ — browse to any .declare file's URL and the server compiles and returns the running app.
Write a program to my-apps/hello.declare and browse to http://127.0.0.1:8200/my-apps/hello.declare — the program URL is the app's address.
Recompile the runtime and compiler (tsc) — needed only after editing the toolchain's own .ts sources, never for writing Declare programs.
Climb the ladder — structure → resolution → analysis → boot (add --assert / --states / --baselines for behavior and visual rungs). Stops at the first failure; every diagnostic names its fix.
Rewrite to the one house style (canon). --check exits 1 on drift (the CI gate); no flag prints to stdout.

maintaining.title: Maintaining the system (after changing toolchain sources or docs)

steps.cmd: npm run build && node tools/internal/build-compiler.mjs && node tools/internal/build-boot.mjs && node tools/internal/doc/extract.mjs && node tools/internal/doc/assemble.mjs && node tools/internal/prewarm.mjs && node tools/internal/bake-homepage-crawler.mjs
steps.description: The regeneration chain, in its load-bearing order: compile → bundles → doc model → link registry → spine projections → prewarmed artifacts → baked page. Run after any .ts, docs/, or registry change.
Every declare-docs: symbolic link in the corpus resolves against the generated registry.
The three spine projections (declare-model.json, the marker-injected doc tables, the skill inventory) match a fresh assembly of the live registries.
Precompile to a self-contained production bundle (~45KB gz + your program). --canvas selects the canvas renderer; --debug keeps positions.
Also bake the crawled document (every linked location's content) into the built page for crawlers.
Append ?extract to any program URL to see the document a crawler gets.

View.x.name: x
View.x.doc:
The horizontal offset within the parent, in pixels. Honoured only while the parent
imposes no `layout` — **a layout overwrites `x` every pass**, so use it for absolute
placement (the layout-none default) and switch to `layout` for arrangement; don't fight
one with the other.

docSegs.md:
The horizontal offset within the parent, in pixels. Honoured only while the parent
imposes no `layout` — **a layout overwrites `x` every pass**, so use it for absolute
placement (the layout-none default) and switch to `layout` for arrangement; don't fight
one with the other.

View.y.name: y
View.y.doc:
The vertical offset within the parent — the twin of `x`, and likewise overwritten by a
parent `layout`.

docSegs.md:
The vertical offset within the parent — the twin of `x`, and likewise overwritten by a
parent `layout`.

View.width.name: width
View.width.doc:
The box's width, in **pixels** (`Length`). Defaults to `0`, so a container with no
width set collapses — give it one, a `{ }` constraint, or `100%` (parent-relative).
Set it live and children constrained to it reflow in the same frame; there is no
re-layout call. To size *to* content instead, constrain it: `width = { Math.min(contentWidth, 480) }`.

docSegs.md:
The box's width, in **pixels** (`Length`). Defaults to `0`, so a container with no
width set collapses — give it one, a `{ }` constraint, or `100%` (parent-relative).
Set it live and children constrained to it reflow in the same frame; there is no
re-layout call. To size *to* content instead, constrain it: `width = { Math.min(contentWidth, 480) }`.

View.height.name: height
View.height.doc:
The box's height in pixels (`Length`), mirroring `width`. `0` by default. A `Text`
left unsized takes its natural measured height, so you usually set `height` only to
clip or to drive a layout.

docSegs.md:
The box's height in pixels (`Length`), mirroring `width`. `0` by default. A `Text`
left unsized takes its natural measured height, so you usually set `height` only to
clip or to drive a layout.

View.fill.name: fill
View.fill.doc:
What paints the box: a solid `Color` or a `gradient(…)` — the one slot, subsuming a
plain background color. `null` (the default) paints **nothing** — an unfilled box is
invisible but still lays out and still catches clicks. In a `[ ]` literal a color is
`#RRGGBB`; inside a `{ }` body it is `0xRRGGBB` (the one place the spelling differs).

```declare
View [ fill = { gradient("90deg", 0x1E2A36, 0x0B141B) } ]
```

docSegs.md:
What paints the box: a solid `Color` or a `gradient(…)` — the one slot, subsuming a

View.cornerRadius.name: cornerRadius
View.cornerRadius.doc:
Rounds the **painted** box by this many pixels (default `0`, square). It shapes fill,
border, and shadow — but not hit-testing or clipping: the box stays a rectangle for
layout and clicks. To clip children to the rounded shape, set `clip = true` as well.

docSegs.md:
Rounds the **painted** box by this many pixels (default `0`, square). It shapes fill,
border, and shadow — but not hit-testing or clipping: the box stays a rectangle for
layout and clicks. To clip children to the rounded shape, set `clip = true` as well.

View.stroke.name: stroke
View.stroke.doc:
A border drawn **inside** the box (`stroke(width, color)`), so it never enlarges the
layout rectangle — the box stays the one geometry fact. `null` by default. Chosen over
CSS's `border` precisely so a bordered view and an unbordered one occupy the same space.

docSegs.md:
A border drawn **inside** the box (`stroke(width, color)`), so it never enlarges the
layout rectangle — the box stays the one geometry fact. `null` by default. Chosen over
CSS's `border` precisely so a bordered view and an unbordered one occupy the same space.

View.shadow.name: shadow
View.shadow.doc:
A drop shadow on the box (`shadow(dx, dy, blur, color)`), the CSS box-shadow shape
minus spread. `null` by default. The glyph equivalent on `Text` is `textShadow`.

docSegs.md:
A drop shadow on the box (`shadow(dx, dy, blur, color)`), the CSS box-shadow shape
minus spread. `null` by default. The glyph equivalent on `Text` is `textShadow`.

View.visible.name: visible
View.visible.doc:
Whether the view renders and participates in layout-that-skips-invisibles (default
`true`). A `false` view is fully inert — no paint, no hit-testing — but still
constructed, so toggling it is cheap and keeps its state.

docSegs.md:
Whether the view renders and participates in layout-that-skips-invisibles (default
`true`). A `false` view is fully inert — no paint, no hit-testing — but still
constructed, so toggling it is cheap and keeps its state.

View.opacity.name: opacity
View.opacity.doc:
Whole-view alpha, `0`…`1` (default `1`). Applies to the view **and its subtree** as a
group, so a fading panel fades its contents with it. Not `prevailing`: its effect
already composes down the render tree, so a followed copy would apply it twice.

docSegs.md:
Whole-view alpha, `0`…`1` (default `1`). Applies to the view **and its subtree** as a
group, so a fading panel fades its contents with it. Not `prevailing`: its effect
already composes down the render tree, so a followed copy would apply it twice.

View.scale.name: scale
View.scale.doc:
A uniform **paint** transform — the view's subtree renders scaled about its pivot, never
re-laid-out (like `opacity`, it changes pixels, not geometry), and hit-testing follows the
visible result. Pair with `pivotX`/`pivotY` to choose the center; `1` is unscaled.

docSegs.md:
A uniform **paint** transform — the view's subtree renders scaled about its pivot, never
re-laid-out (like `opacity`, it changes pixels, not geometry), and hit-testing follows the
visible result. Pair with `pivotX`/`pivotY` to choose the center; `1` is unscaled.

View.pivotX.name: pivotX
View.pivotX.doc:
The horizontal center of `scale`, in the view's own coordinates. Defaults to the origin; set
both `pivotX`/`pivotY` to scale about the middle rather than the top-left.

docSegs.md:
The horizontal center of `scale`, in the view's own coordinates. Defaults to the origin; set
both `pivotX`/`pivotY` to scale about the middle rather than the top-left.

View.pivotY.name: pivotY
View.pivotY.doc: The vertical pivot — the twin of `pivotX`.

docSegs.md: The vertical pivot — the twin of `pivotX`.

View.clip.name: clip
View.clip.doc:
Clips the subtree to a shape. `clip = true` clips to the view's **own box**
(reactively on width/height) — the common case; a `Shape` value clips to an arbitrary
path. `null`/unset (default) draws children unclipped, even outside the box. Kept
explicit (not implied by `cornerRadius`) so clipping is pay-per-use.

View.scrolls.name: scrolls
View.scrolls.doc:
Makes the view scroll its overflowing content: it clips to its box and scrolls the
vertical overflow, exposing `scrollY`. Fixed chrome comes free — make it a **sibling**
of the scroller, not a child. Default `false`. Both backends realize it natively and
present the same model (`scrollY` updates the same way either way); only the overscroll
*feel* differs, where the platform can do better. On DOM the OS owns the scroll — overlay
scrollbar, momentum, and rubber-band overscroll *contained* to this pane, so it bounces on
its own edges and never chains to the page, and sibling panes overscroll independently. On
canvas the runtime manages the offset (clip+translate+wheel), as a single element must.

View.tip.name: tip
View.tip.doc:
The tooltip text — the layer system's floor (one attribute at the use site): a non-empty
`tip` makes this view hover-interactive, and after the theme's delay the auto-provided
`Tooltip` singleton shows the text beside it. Placement, delay, and size are theme data
(`tooltipPlacement` below | above | pointer, `tooltipDelay`, `tooltipSize`) — so Cupertino
tips appear near the cursor after ~1s at 11px (the macOS help tag), Redmond's above the
control (WinUI), Mountain View's below at ~500ms (M3) — always flipped and clamped inside
the app. Moving between tip-carrying controls while a tip is up retargets instantly; a
press dismisses. Look comes from `tooltipBg` / `tooltipText` / `tooltipLine`. `""` (the
default) = no tip.

View.scrollsX.name: scrollsX
View.scrollsX.doc:
Like `scrolls`, but for **horizontal** overflow: the view clips to its box and scrolls content
wider than it, exposing the offset. Use it for a paging strip; `scrolls` is the vertical case.

docSegs.md:
Like `scrolls`, but for **horizontal** overflow: the view clips to its box and scrolls content
wider than it, exposing the offset. Use it for a paging strip; `scrolls` is the vertical case.

View.scrollY.name: scrollY
View.scrollY.doc:
The current vertical scroll offset in pixels of a `scrolls` view — **read it** for
scroll-driven effects (a fading header, reveals, parallax): `opacity = { 1 - app.scrollY / 200 }`.

docSegs.md:
The current vertical scroll offset in pixels of a `scrolls` view — **read it** for
scroll-driven effects (a fading header, reveals, parallax): `opacity = { 1 - app.scrollY / 200 }`.

View.textColor.name: textColor
View.textColor.doc:
The glyph color `Text` renders with — a `prevailing` styling slot declared on `View`
so **any** container can provide it and the whole subtree inherits, live. Unset, it
follows the nearest ancestor that sets it (default `0x000000`). Set it on a panel to
retint all text beneath without touching each `Text`.

docSegs.md:
The glyph color `Text` renders with — a `prevailing` styling slot declared on `View`
so **any** container can provide it and the whole subtree inherits, live. Unset, it
follows the nearest ancestor that sets it (default `0x000000`). Set it on a panel to
retint all text beneath without touching each `Text`.

View.fontSize.name: fontSize
View.fontSize.doc:
Prevailing font size in pixels (default `16`), inherited by descendant `Text` exactly
like `textColor`. Set once on a container to size a whole region's text.

docSegs.md:
Prevailing font size in pixels (default `16`), inherited by descendant `Text` exactly
like `textColor`. Set once on a container to size a whole region's text.

View.fontFamily.name: fontFamily
View.fontFamily.doc:
The prevailing font **fallback list**, read at each descendant `Text` — `[Brand, "system-ui", "sans-serif"]`.
A bare name resolves to a declared `font`; the first entry that resolves wins, so end
with a generic. Prevailing, so setting it on a container refaces the whole region.

A bare name resolves to a declared `font`; the first entry that resolves wins, so end
with a generic. Prevailing, so setting it on a container refaces the whole region.

View.fontWeight.name: fontWeight
View.fontWeight.doc:
The prevailing weight — one of the `thin`…`black` tokens (`normal`/`bold` alias 400/700).
The token also **picks the matching face** when a `font` declares several, so weight and
face never drift apart.

View.fontWeight.type: thin | extralight | light | regular | normal | medium | semibold | bold | extrabold | black

View.letterSpacing.name: letterSpacing
View.letterSpacing.doc:
Prevailing tracking, in **px** (not em) — `0` is the font's natural advances. Prevailing,
so a heading container can loosen all its text at once.

docSegs.md:
Prevailing tracking, in **px** (not em) — `0` is the font's natural advances. Prevailing,
so a heading container can loosen all its text at once.

View.headingColor.name: headingColor
View.headingColor.doc:
**Prevailing.** The color of `Markdown`/`RichText` headings, overriding `textColor` for heading
runs only; absent, headings follow `textColor`.

docSegs.md:
**Prevailing.** The color of `Markdown`/`RichText` headings, overriding `textColor` for heading
runs only; absent, headings follow `textColor`.

View.headingWeight.name: headingWeight
View.headingWeight.doc:
**Prevailing.** The font weight for rich-text headings — the heading-specific counterpart to
`fontWeight`.

docSegs.md:
**Prevailing.** The font weight for rich-text headings — the heading-specific counterpart to

View.linkColor.name: linkColor
View.linkColor.doc: **Prevailing.** The color of links in rich text; absent, links use the theme's accent.

docSegs.md: **Prevailing.** The color of links in rich text; absent, links use the theme's accent.

View.codeColor.name: codeColor
View.codeColor.doc: **Prevailing.** The text color of inline and fenced code in rich text.

docSegs.md: **Prevailing.** The text color of inline and fenced code in rich text.

View.codeSize.name: codeSize
View.codeSize.doc:
**Prevailing.** The font size for code regions — one value driving inline code, fenced blocks,
and `<pre>` alike, so a document's monospace stays uniform.

docSegs.md:
**Prevailing.** The font size for code regions — one value driving inline code, fenced blocks,
and `<pre>` alike, so a document's monospace stays uniform.

View.codeFamily.name: codeFamily
View.codeFamily.doc: **Prevailing.** The monospace family for code regions — a fallback list, like `fontFamily`.

docSegs.md: **Prevailing.** The monospace family for code regions — a fallback list, like `fontFamily`.

View.codeBackground.name: codeBackground
View.codeBackground.doc: **Prevailing.** The fill behind fenced code blocks; absent, code carries no box.

docSegs.md: **Prevailing.** The fill behind fenced code blocks; absent, code carries no box.

View.codeRule.name: codeRule
View.codeRule.doc: **Prevailing.** The color of a fenced code block's left accent rule.

docSegs.md: **Prevailing.** The color of a fenced code block's left accent rule.

View.richTextLayout.name: richTextLayout
View.richTextLayout.doc:
**Prevailing.** A per-block-type geometry map for `RichText`/`Markdown` — caller-controlled
measure and spacing per block kind (prose narrower than code, say), so one base renders both
tight code and wide prose.

measure and spacing per block kind (prose narrower than code, say), so one base renders both
tight code and wide prose.

View.theme.name: theme
View.theme.doc:
A prevailing record of design tokens, read inside `{ }` as `theme.accent`, `theme.muted`,
etc. Provide it on a container and the subtree styles off it — the escape from hard-coded
colours when you don't want a full `stylesheet`.

etc. Provide it on a container and the subtree styles off it — the escape from hard-coded
colours when you don't want a full `stylesheet`.

View.selectable.name: selectable
View.selectable.doc:
**Prevailing.** `selectable = true` on a container makes all its `Text` — including a
`Markdown`'s rendered runs — selectable and copyable; **off by default**, so UI chrome never
becomes accidentally selectable. Set it once high over a region of prose.

becomes accidentally selectable. Set it once high over a region of prose.

View.styles.name: styles
View.styles.doc:
An ordered list of `style` bundles applied at **construction** — **static in v1**: unlike
`stylesheet`, reassigning `styles` after build does nothing. For a reskin that changes
live, use `stylesheet` instead.

docSegs.md:
An ordered list of `style` bundles applied at **construction** — **static in v1**: unlike
`stylesheet`, reassigning `styles` after build does nothing. For a reskin that changes
live, use `stylesheet` instead.

View.stylesheet.name: stylesheet
View.stylesheet.doc:
The prevailing stylesheet: provide one anywhere and that whole subtree reskins; swap it
and the subtree re-styles in a single settle. The reactive counterpart to the static
`styles` list.

docSegs.md:
The prevailing stylesheet: provide one anywhere and that whole subtree reskins; swap it
and the subtree re-styles in a single settle. The reactive counterpart to the static
`styles` list.

View.layout.name: layout
View.layout.doc:
How this view arranges its children — a reactive `Layout` attribute, not a child and
not the container's type. Defaults to none (absolute `x`/`y`). Swap or animate it and
the arrangement transitions continuously: `layout: SimpleLayout [ axis = y, spacing = 10 ]`.
Set `layout = null` for explicit none.

Set `layout = null` for explicit none.

View.datapath.name: datapath
View.datapath.doc:
The data cursor (language §9): sets the place in a dataset that this view and its
descendants read relative to. Write it as a `:path` (relative to the inherited
cursor), `:arr[]` to **replicate** this view once per array element, or a `{ }`
expression yielding a place. Descendants read with their own relative `:paths`.

View.focusable.name: focusable
View.focusable.doc:
Makes the view a keyboard **tab stop**. Traversal order is the view tree — there is no
numeric tabindex; override `tabOrder()` to reorder within a container.

docSegs.md:
Makes the view a keyboard **tab stop**. Traversal order is the view tree — there is no
numeric tabindex; override `tabOrder()` to reorder within a container.

View.focustrap.name: focustrap
View.focustrap.doc:
Marks a self-contained focus group: Tab cycles within it and escapes at the boundary
(firing `escapeFocus`). For a modal or menu whose focus must not leak to the page behind.

docSegs.md:
Marks a self-contained focus group: Tab cycles within it and escapes at the boundary
(firing `escapeFocus`). For a modal or menu whose focus must not leak to the page behind.

View.anchor.name: anchor
View.anchor.doc:
Names this view as a **reveal target** for a location's `@name` suffix
(`#guide/22-reach@intro` scrolls to the view with `anchor = "intro"`). The `<a name>`
lineage, reborn reactive: the anchor namespace is named views (this attribute) plus
heading slugs inside rendered rich text — a heading needs nothing from you. Resolution
prefers views over slugs, preorder-first.

View.contentWidth.name: contentWidth
View.contentWidth.doc:
**Read-only** intrinsic: the width of this view's visible children's bounding box —
the auto-extent, surfaced. A constraint may read it (`width = { Math.min(contentWidth, 480) }`)
to size to content with a cap; assigning it is a compile error.

**Read-only** intrinsic: the width of this view's visible children's bounding box —
the auto-extent, surfaced. A constraint may read it (`width = { Math.min(contentWidth, 480) }`)
to size to content with a cap; assigning it is a compile error.

View.contentHeight.name: contentHeight
View.contentHeight.doc:
**Read-only** intrinsic mirroring `contentWidth` on the vertical axis — the measured
extent of the subtree, for sizing a container to its content.

docSegs.md:
**Read-only** intrinsic mirroring `contentWidth` on the vertical axis — the measured
extent of the subtree, for sizing a container to its content.

View.method.attach.name: attach
View.method.attach.signature: attach(backend: RenderBackend, parentSurface: Surface | null, before: Surface | null): void

View.method.scrollIntoView.name: scrollIntoView
View.method.scrollIntoView.doc:
Scrolls this view into the visible region of its nearest `scrolls` ancestor (or the page),
aligning its top to the viewport top — the imperative partner of the declarative jump-index
pattern. Both backends realize it natively (DOM `scrollIntoView`, canvas clamps the scroll
ancestor's `scrollOffset`). A no-op if nothing above it scrolls.

docSegs.md:
Scrolls this view into the visible region of its nearest `scrolls` ancestor (or the page),
aligning its top to the viewport top — the imperative partner of the declarative jump-index
pattern. Both backends realize it natively (DOM `scrollIntoView`, canvas clamps the scroll
ancestor's `scrollOffset`). A no-op if nothing above it scrolls.

View.event.click.name: click
View.event.click.doc:
Fires when the pointer presses **and** releases on the same view (a true click, not a
stray press) — answered by an `onClick()` handler. The primary interaction event;
`mouseDown`/`mouseUp`/`mouseMove` are there when you need the raw phases.

```declare
View [ width = 80, height = 44, cornerRadius = 6, fill = gainsboro, onClick() { fill = 0x4169E1 } ]
```

View.event.mouseDown.name: mouseDown
View.event.mouseDown.doc:
The pointer pressed on the view — the raw press phase. Prefer `click` (press **and**
release on the same view) unless you need the phases apart, e.g. to begin a drag.

docSegs.md:
The pointer pressed on the view — the raw press phase. Prefer `click` (press **and**
release on the same view) unless you need the phases apart, e.g. to begin a drag.

View.event.mouseUp.name: mouseUp
View.event.mouseUp.doc:
The pointer released. While a press is captured (it began on this view) `mouseUp` still
fires **here even if the release lands off the box** — the drag-release guarantee, so a
slider freezes its value wherever the finger lifts.

View.name: View
View.doc:
The base of every visual thing in Declare — a rectangular **box** with a position, a
size, and decoration (fill, corner radius, border, shadow). Everything you see
descends from `View`: `Text`, `Image`, your own `class … extends View`. A plain
`View` is a colored box; give it children and it becomes a container.

Its geometry is set two ways that read the same: literal (`width = 200`) or a reactive
`{ }` constraint the runtime keeps true (`width = { parent.width }`). Children nest
inside the brackets, so the source shape mirrors the visual shape; *how* they are
arranged is the `layout` attribute, not the container's type.

```declare
View [ width = 200, height = 120, fill = white, cornerRadius = 8,
  Text [ text = "hello", x = 12, y = 12 ]
]
```

responsive layout at any depth. Assigning it is a compile error.

App.dark.name: dark
App.dark.doc:
**Read-only.** The OS color-scheme flag — `true` under `prefers-color-scheme: dark`, kept
live as the system theme flips. Theme off it: `theme = { app.dark ? darkTokens() : lightTokens() }`.
It is only the OS signal; when you offer a Light/Dark/Auto control, keep your own mode
attribute and read `app.dark` as the "auto" case.

App.location.name: location
App.location.doc:
The app's slice of the URL — the **fragment**, as one two-way reactive string
(docs/system-design/location.md). The host seeds it from the URL *before first settle* (a deep link
is just an initial state), mirrors app writes outward (one history entry per changed
settle), and writes it back on back/forward — so navigation, deep links, and the back
button are all the same thing: a `location` write your constraints re-derive from. The
app owns the grammar: read it (`mode = { app.location.split("/")[0] }`), write it to
navigate (`app.location = "why"`). The declared initial is the **default location** —
the URL stays clean while the app is at it. A trailing `@name` reveals the named view
or heading after the settle it causes.

```declare
App [ location = "home",
    why: View [ visible = { app.location == "why" } ],
]
```

App.minWidth.name: minWidth
App.minWidth.doc:
The width below which the app **stops adapting** — in a narrower host it holds this width and
the stage pans natively (the browser scrolls it), rather than reflowing into an unusable shape.
Declare a floor instead of writing `Math.max` clamps into size constraints.

App.minHeight.name: minHeight

App.method.navigate.name: navigate
App.method.navigate.doc:
Navigate the host **out** of the app: `app.navigate("https://…")` for an external link, or
`app.navigate("some/app.declare")` for another program (resolved against the distro root). It
is a service **method**, not an attribute — call it from a handler; `app.navigate = …` is an
error.

App.name: App
App.doc:
The root of every Declare program, and its reactive **environment**. There is exactly
one, at the top of the tree; reach it from any depth with the **`app` noun**
(`app.hostWidth`, `app.scrollY`) rather than a fragile `parent` chain. It extends `View`,
so it is also the outermost box — and it **fills its host by default** (its `width`/`height`
default to `hostWidth`/`hostHeight`), so a plain app is full-window and an aspect-locked
one reads the host extent. The attributes below are fed by the runtime from the window (or
the embedding element): you **read** them, you never set them.

```declare
App [ fill = white,
    header: View [ width = { app.hostWidth }, opacity = { 1 - app.scrollY / 200 } ],
]
```

Text.text.doc:
The string to display. Literal, or a `{ }` constraint that recomputes as its
dependencies change — `text = { classroot.label }` re-renders the moment `label` does,
with no subscription to wire.

Text.textShadow.name: textShadow
Text.textShadow.doc:
A drop shadow on the glyphs — the same `shadow(dx, dy, blur, color)` value as the box

Text.wrap.name: wrap
Text.wrap.doc:
Whether a width-bounded run wraps to multiple lines (default `true`). Set `wrap = false`
to force a single line that overflows instead. Wrapping is reactive: narrow the
bounding width and the run re-flows in the same frame. Pairs with `textAlign`.

Text.textAlign.name: textAlign
Text.textAlign.doc:

Text.name: Text
Text.doc:
A run of text, sized by native browser metrics when you don't give it a width or
height — so a bare `Text [ text = "hi" ]` is exactly as wide and tall as its glyphs.
Its **style** is not on `Text`: `textColor`, `fontSize`, `fontFamily`, and
`fontWeight` are `prevailing` slots on `View`, so any ancestor provides them and this
run renders with the effective values. That is why restyling a region's text means
setting those on the container, not on each `Text`.

```declare
View [ textColor = royalblue, fontSize = 14,
  Text [ text = "inherits the panel's style" ]
]
```

changes. A stateless helper in a `script { }` beats wrapping a class around one
computed URL.

Image.stretches.doc:
How the bitmap fills a box whose size differs from the image's natural size: `none`
(default — natural size, no scaling), `width`, `height`, or `both`. The first built-in
enum attribute — `stretches = both` scales the picture to the box on both axes.

Image.name: Image
Image.doc:
An async-loaded bitmap. Set `source` to a URL; the image loads in the background and,
unless you constrain its size, adopts its natural pixel dimensions once loaded (so
`width`/`height` left unset "pop" to the real size on load — constrain them if you need
a stable box before the bytes arrive).

```declare
Image [ source = { weatherIcon(:code) }, width = 52, height = 52 ]
```

weight/size/tracking come from the ambient `fontWeight`/`fontSize`/`letterSpacing`.)

RichText.event.link.name: link
RichText.event.link.doc:
A link (`[text](url)` in Markdown, `<a href>` in HTMLText) was activated — you get its `href`,
and you decide what it means: scroll to an anchor, set an in-app route, or open externally. The
runtime only delivers the click. Left unhandled, a link falls back to `app.navigate` (so
external links work with no wiring); declaring `onLink` overrides that. Modifier/middle clicks
still open a new tab natively. Same on both backends.

and you decide what it means: scroll to an anchor, set an in-app route, or open externally. The
runtime only delivers the click. Left unhandled, a link falls back to `app.navigate` (so

RichText.name: RichText
RichText.doc:
The abstract family of **flowing, structured, styled text** — the shared base of `Markdown`
and `HTMLText`. You never write `RichText [ ]` directly (like `Layout`, it names no format);
you write one of its two concrete formats, which differ *only* in how they parse their
source. Everything else — how it flows, wraps, stacks, and styles — lives here.

**Styling follows the ordinary text properties.** A rich-text block's **body** obeys the same
inherited slots a `Text` does — `fontSize`, `fontWeight`, `textColor`, `letterSpacing`,
`lineHeight` — so prose is no longer a sealed world: set `fontWeight = semibold` on a
container and the prose body below is semibold. Its **structure** (headings, code, links,
list spacing) comes from a built-in house style that looks right with zero config and follows
light/dark on its own; the parts you commonly re-theme are their own inherited slots —
`headingColor`, `headingWeight`, `linkColor`, `codeColor` — each cascading like `fontSize`.

Markdown.text.doc:
The Markdown source — a literal, or a `{ }` constraint that re-parses whenever it changes
(a streamed response, an editor's live text).

docSegs.md:
The Markdown source — a literal, or a `{ }` constraint that re-parses whenever it changes

Markdown.name: Markdown
Markdown.doc:
Rich content authored in **Markdown** — point it at a string and it parses (headings, lists,
tables, inline code, links) into a stack of real, wrapped, prose-styled **views**, not a
foreign HTML blob (so it lays out, sizes, and renders identically on both backends). Literal
or computed: `text = { article.body }` re-parses and re-renders reactively, so it handles
streamed or live-edited Markdown. A `RichText`, so it carries all the shared prose styling
(`lineHeight`, `bodyColor`, `onLink`, and the ambient text properties).

```declare
Markdown [ width = { parent.width }, text = { :body } ]
```

tables, inline code, links) into a stack of real, wrapped, prose-styled **views**, not a
foreign HTML blob (so it lays out, sizes, and renders identically on both backends). Literal
or computed: `text = { article.body }` re-parses and re-renders reactively, so it handles
streamed or live-edited Markdown. A `RichText`, so it carries all the shared prose styling
(`lineHeight`, `bodyColor`, `onLink`, and the ambient text properties).

example.name: Markdown
example.source:
// Markdown.declare — author prose in Markdown; it renders as real, wrapped views

App [
    Markdown [ x = 28, y = 20, width = 400, text = """
# Declare

A **declarative** UI language:

- reactive `{ }` constraints
- native layout, both backends
- text authored in Markdown
""" ],
    ]

HTMLText.html.name: html
HTMLText.html.doc:
The HTML source — a literal, or a `{ }` constraint that re-parses whenever it changes (a
fetched document, a live-edited field). Only `<a href>` and `<span class>` (see `accents`)
are read; every other attribute is ignored.

docSegs.md:
The HTML source — a literal, or a `{ }` constraint that re-parses whenever it changes (a
fetched document, a live-edited field). Only `<a href>` and `<span class>` (see `accents`)
are read; every other attribute is ignored.

HTMLText.unsupported.name: unsupported
HTMLText.unsupported.doc:
What a tag **outside the whitelist** does — the reason this is safe for loaded content:

- `strip` (the default) — the unknown tag is **unwrapped**: it is dropped but its text is
  kept, so `<marquee>hi</marquee>` renders as `hi`. `<script>` / `<style>` are dropped whole
  (content and all).
- `error` — the first unsupported tag **throws**, naming it. Use this when unexpected markup
  should be a hard failure rather than silently pruned.

(`lineHeight`, `bodyColor`, and `onLink` — the shared prose styling and the link event — come
from the `RichText` base.)

HTMLText.accents.name: accents
HTMLText.accents.doc:
The one styling hook, and the only attribute read besides `href`: a map of **name → Fill** that a `` in the HTML can reference to paint its glyphs — a gradient or a solid — so an accent word can flow *inside* a sentence. The content only *names* a fill the app defines; it never carries CSS itself, so this stays safe for loaded HTML (an unknown class just renders as plain text). This is how one flowing string carries a gradient word: ```declare HTMLText [ width = { parent.width }, scale = 1.6, accents = { { accent: gradient("90deg", 0x4C8DFF, 0x37E0C8) } }, html = "

Declare is the UI language for the AI era.

" ] ``` Size follows the prose stylesheet (heading level × `scale`), not a `fontSize` — the accent is about *which* run is painted, not how big it is.

docSegs.md:
The one styling hook, and the only attribute read besides `href`: a map of **name → Fill**
that a `<span class="name">` in the HTML can reference to paint its glyphs — a gradient or a
solid — so an accent word can flow *inside* a sentence. The content only *names* a fill the
app defines; it never carries CSS itself, so this stays safe for loaded HTML (an unknown
class just renders as plain text). This is how one flowing string carries a gradient word:

code.source:
App [ HTMLText [ width = { parent.width }, scale = 1.6, accents = { { accent: gradient("90deg", 0x4C8DFF, 0x37E0C8) } }, html = "

Declare is the UI language for the AI era.

" ] ]

docSegs.md:

HTMLText.name: HTMLText
HTMLText.doc:
Renders rich content authored — or **loaded** — as a small, whitelisted subset of **HTML**. The sibling of `Markdown`: it parses the string at render time into the same stack of real, wrapped, prose-styled **views** (identical on both backends), so paragraphs and headings get native, contiguous text selection for free. Supported tags are the text-shaped ones — `p`, `h1`–`h6`, `b`/`strong`, `i`/`em`, `code`, `s`/`del`, `a`, `br`, `ul`/`ol`/`li`, `blockquote`, `pre`, `hr`, `span`, `div` — and nothing else. This is **not** the `HTML[ ]` island (that mounts arbitrary host DOM you manage yourself): `HTMLText` is *sanitized rich text*, with defined, safe behaviour on anything outside the whitelist — so it is the right choice for content that arrives from data. `html = { post.body }` re-parses and re-renders reactively. ```declare HTMLText [ width = { parent.width }, html = "

Notice

Loaded content with bold, italic, and a link.

" ] ```

" ] ]

DOMIsland.slot.name: slot
DOMIsland.slot.doc:
The host key — reflected onto the element as `data-declare-slot`, so the host can locate this box
and mount foreign content into it. Set it to `""` to mount nothing (a closed island); flip it
to show or swap the tenant reactively.

docSegs.md:
The host key — reflected onto the element as `data-declare-slot`, so the host can locate this box
and mount foreign content into it. Set it to `""` to mount nothing (a closed island); flip it
to show or swap the tenant reactively.

DOMIsland.name: DOMIsland
DOMIsland.doc:
A foreign-content island: a leaf `View` that Declare **sizes and positions like any view**
(it obeys constraints and layout), but whose *interior* is host-managed DOM — an `<iframe>`,
a `<textarea>`, a `<video>`, a map widget. It is the **one sanctioned escape to raw DOM**,
kept behind a named view so `{ }` bodies stay DOM-free. The DOM backend reflects `slot` as a
`data-declare-slot` attribute; the host finds that box and mounts content into it, with Declare's
width/height driving the tenant's size and no coordinate sync to maintain.

```declare
preview: DOMIsland [ width = { parent.width }, height = 300, slot = "run:demo" ]
```

TextInput.text.name: text
TextInput.text.doc:
The field's contents. With `text <-> :path` it is the **draft** of a two-way edit session over
a dataset record (see the intro). Otherwise: **bind** it (`text = { model }`) for a
**controlled** field — an edit that diverges from the binding reverts — or leave it unbound
(or seed it with `initial`) for a field the user edits freely.

**controlled** field — an edit that diverges from the binding reverts — or leave it unbound
(or seed it with `initial`) for a field the user edits freely.

TextInput.placeholder.doc: The grey prompt shown while the field is empty.

TextInput.multiline.name: multiline
TextInput.multiline.doc:
Makes it a multi-line area rather than a single line — and then **Return inserts a newline**
instead of firing `enter`.

TextInput.wrap.name: wrap
TextInput.wrap.doc:
For a `multiline` field, whether long lines wrap (`true`) or scroll horizontally (`false`) —
a code field wants `false`.

TextInput.padding.doc: Inner padding around the text, in px.

TextInput.initial.name: initial
TextInput.initial.doc:
An **uncontrolled seed** — React's `defaultValue` to `text`'s `value`: `text` starts at
`initial`, then holds the user's edits. For a field pre-filled from a value that must stay
freely writable (an editor seeded with source). A **bound** `text` is the controlled form
instead; **don't set both** — pick controlled *or* seeded.

TextInput.method.attach.name: attach
TextInput.method.attach.signature: attach(backend: RenderBackend, parentSurface: Surface | null): void

TextInput.event.input.name: input
TextInput.event.input.doc:
Fires on every edit, carrying the new text — for live validation or search-as-you-type. On a
`<->` field the draft is already committed (per `commitOn`) by the time this runs.

docSegs.md:

`<->` field the draft is already committed (per `commitOn`) by the time this runs.

TextInput.event.enter.name: enter
TextInput.event.enter.doc:
Fires when the user submits a single-line field (Return). On a `multiline` field Return
makes a newline and this never fires.

docSegs.md:
Fires when the user submits a single-line field (Return). On a `multiline` field Return
makes a newline and this never fires.

TextInput.name: TextInput
TextInput.doc:
An editable text field, realized as a **native** editable element (a DOM input in-box, a
positioned overlay on canvas) so caret, selection, IME, and accessibility are the
platform's, not reimplemented. It fires `input` on every edit and `enter` on a single-line
submit, and inherits View's focus and keyboard events.

It is also the first **Editor**: bind `text <-> :path` to edit a dataset record two-way — the
field reads the datapath, commits edits back into the dataset, and reseeds when the cursor
moves to a new record. The dataset owns the committed value; the field owns the *edit session*
— its draft plus `valid` / `error` / `dirty`. Supply a `validate(v)` method for a domain rule
beyond the schema type (return an error message, or `null` when valid); an invalid draft is
held and never written.

```declare
App [ fill = white, textColor = black,
    contact: Dataset { { "email": "ada@example.com" } },
    form: View [ x = 24, y = 24, datapath = { app.contact.value },
        email: TextInput [ width = 240, height = 30, padding = 6, cornerRadius = 6,
            fill = gainsboro,
            text <-> :email,
            validate(v) { return v.includes("@") ? null : "not an email" } ] ],
    ]
```

positioned overlay on canvas) so caret, selection, IME, and accessibility are the
platform's, not reimplemented. It fires `input` on every edit and `enter` on a single-line
submit, and inherits View's focus and keyboard events.

It is also the first **Editor**: bind `text <-> :path` to edit a dataset record two-way — the

moves to a new record. The dataset owns the committed value; the field owns the *edit session*

held and never written.

code.source:
App [ fill = white, textColor = black,
    contact: Dataset { { "email": "ada@example.com" } },
    form: View [ x = 24, y = 24, datapath = { app.contact.value },

            fill = gainsboro,
            text <-> :email,
            validate(v) { return v.includes("@") ? null : "not an email" } ] ],
    ]

SimpleLayout.axis.name: axis
SimpleLayout.axis.doc:
The stacking direction — `x` lays children left-to-right (a row), `y` top-to-bottom (a
column). You always name it; there is no "obvious" default for a stack.

docSegs.md:
The stacking direction — `x` lays children left-to-right (a row), `y` top-to-bottom (a
column). You always name it; there is no "obvious" default for a stack.

SimpleLayout.spacing.name: spacing
SimpleLayout.spacing.doc:
The gap between adjacent children, in px. **Negative values overlap them** — stacked
cards, an avatar pile. Invisible children are skipped, so the gap closes automatically
when one hides rather than leaving a hole.

docSegs.md:
The gap between adjacent children, in px. **Negative values overlap them** — stacked
cards, an avatar pile. Invisible children are skipped, so the gap closes automatically
when one hides rather than leaving a hole.

SimpleLayout.name: SimpleLayout
SimpleLayout.doc:
The stacking layout: arranges a view's children in a line along `axis`, `spacing` px
apart, skipping invisible ones. You set it as the view's `layout` **attribute** — not a
child (OpenLaszlo's form), not the container's type (SwiftUI/Flutter's) — which is what
lets you **swap or animate it** so an arrangement *transitions* instead of jumping. While
it's active it drives the children's positions, so any `x`/`y` you set on them is
overwritten.

```declare
View [ layout: SimpleLayout [ axis = y, spacing = 10 ],
    Text [ text = "one" ],
    Text [ text = "two" ],
]
```

WrappingLayout.spacing.name: spacing
WrappingLayout.spacing.doc: The horizontal gap between items within a row, in px.

docSegs.md: The horizontal gap between items within a row, in px.

WrappingLayout.lineSpacing.name: lineSpacing
WrappingLayout.lineSpacing.doc:
The vertical gap between wrapped rows, in px — **defaults to `spacing`** when unset, so a
single value gives even gaps in both directions and you only set this to make the rows
tighter or looser than the columns.

docSegs.md:
The vertical gap between wrapped rows, in px — **defaults to `spacing`** when unset, so a
single value gives even gaps in both directions and you only set this to make the rows
tighter or looser than the columns.

WrappingLayout.name: WrappingLayout
WrappingLayout.doc:
A horizontal flow that **wraps** to a new row when the view runs out of width — a row
while there's room, rows when there isn't. For tag lists, chip clouds, a toolbar that
reflows. It re-wraps reactively as the view resizes, so binding the view's `width` to its
parent is all it takes to make the flow responsive.

```declare
View [ width = { parent.width }, layout: WrappingLayout [ spacing = 8, lineSpacing = 8 ] ]
```

docSegs.md:
A horizontal flow that **wraps** to a new row when the view runs out of width — a row
while there's room, rows when there isn't. For tag lists, chip clouds, a toolbar that
reflows. It re-wraps reactively as the view resizes, so binding the view's `width` to its
parent is all it takes to make the flow responsive.

example.name: WrappingLayout
example.source:
// WrappingLayout.declare — a horizontal flow of chips that wraps to new rows

class Chip extends View [ height = 30, cornerRadius = 15, fill = steelblue,
    label: string = "",
    Text [ x = 12, y = 7, fontSize = 13, fontWeight = semibold,
        textColor = whitesmoke, text = { classroot.label } ],
    ]


App [
    View [ x = 24, y = 24, width = 320,
        layout: WrappingLayout [ spacing = 10, lineSpacing = 10 ],
        Chip [ width = 74, label = "views" ],
        Chip [ width = 92, label = "reactive" ],
        Chip [ width = 78, label = "layout" ],
        Chip [ width = 68, label = "text" ],
        Chip [ width = 96, label = "animator" ],
        Chip [ width = 66, label = "state" ],
        Chip [ width = 86, label = "dataset" ],
        Chip [ width = 90, label = "markdown" ],
        ],
    ]

TweenLayout.t.name: t
TweenLayout.t.doc:
The interpolation scalar, `0`…`1` — `0` is the "from" arrangement, `1` the "to". Bind it
to a state or animate it; the layout places each child at the blended position, so the
transition is continuous.

docSegs.md:
The interpolation scalar, `0`…`1` — `0` is the "from" arrangement, `1` the "to". Bind it
to a state or animate it; the layout places each child at the blended position, so the
transition is continuous.

TweenLayout.duration.name: duration
TweenLayout.duration.doc:
The duration, in ms, of the built-in retarget animation the base runs when the layout
switches its `from`/`to` targets.

docSegs.md:

TweenLayout.name: TweenLayout
TweenLayout.doc:
The animated-reflow base you **extend** to glide children between two whole layouts
through one scalar `t`. Your subclass supplies a `place()` and its own state; drive `t`
from `0`→`1` with a constraint or an `Animator` and the children slide *between*
arrangements rather than snapping. This is the forcing case for author-written layouts —
a grid that becomes a list, a fan that collapses to a stack.

Dataset.contents.name: contents
Dataset.contents.doc:
Makes the `Dataset` **derived**: a `{ }` constraint (in place of a JSON body) that computes
the value from other reactive state — `matches: Dataset [ contents = { app.filter() } ]`. It
recomputes exactly when what it reads changes, dep-gated like any constraint. Pair a derived
list with `key = :field` on its replicated child so a recompute reconciles by that stable
field (O(changed) instances) instead of by object identity (rebuild-all).

the value from other reactive state — `matches: Dataset [ contents = { app.filter() } ]`. It
recomputes exactly when what it reads changes, dep-gated like any constraint. Pair a derived
list with `key = :field` on its replicated child so a recompute reconciles by that stable
field (O(changed) instances) instead of by object identity (rebuild-all).

Dataset.method.read.name: read
Dataset.method.read.doc:
Tracked region read: `data.read([ "cols" ])` returns the value at that path AND subscribes
the caller to it, so a derivation (or any constraint) that reads through `read` re-runs on an
in-place `.set` edit to that region — the granular counterpart to reading `.value` (which
tracks only a wholesale replacement).

docSegs.md:

the caller to it, so a derivation (or any constraint) that reads through `read` re-runs on an
in-place `.set` edit to that region — the granular counterpart to reading `.value` (which
tracks only a wholesale replacement).

Dataset.method.set.name: set
Dataset.method.set.doc:
Writes `v` at a dotted `path` inside the value (`data.set("cols.0.label", "Mon")`), waking
exactly the readers of that place — the surgical alternative to swapping the whole `.value`.
Creates missing intermediate objects along the path.

Dataset.method.insert.name: insert
Dataset.method.insert.doc:
Splices `v` into the array at `path`, at `index` — every replicated view bound to that array
(`:arr[]`) gains one instance in the same settle, no manual list bookkeeping.

Dataset.method.removeAt.name: removeAt
Dataset.method.removeAt.doc:
Removes and returns the element at `index` of the array at `path`; the replicated instance
for it is torn down in the same settle.

docSegs.md:
Removes and returns the element at `index` of the array at `path`; the replicated instance
for it is torn down in the same settle.

Dataset.method.move.name: move
Dataset.method.move.doc:
Reorders the array at `path`, moving the element at `from` to `to` — the replicated views
follow the new order (a reorder, not a destroy-and-rebuild), so their state rides along.

Dataset.name: Dataset
Dataset.doc:
A named node holding a JSON value — the in-memory data a subtree reads through
**datapaths**. Its data is EITHER a raw `{ }` body (the JSON literal) OR a derived
`contents = { }` constraint; point a view's `datapath` at it (or a slice of it) and
descendants read with relative `:paths`, replicating one instance per array element with
`:arr[]`. It is a **non-visual** node: it lives in the tree as a named member with no box
of its own. For data that arrives over the network, use `DataSource`.

```declare
cal: Dataset { { "days": [], "cols": [] } },
grid: View [ datapath = { classroot.cal.value },
    Day [ datapath = :days[] ],          // one Day per element of cal.value.days
]
```

Read or replace the whole value through `.value` (a reactive slot — writing it wakes
every reader); a whole-value swap re-renders the datapaths that read it in one settle.

App [ fill = white, textColor = black,
    facts: Dataset { { "rows": [
        { "label": "reactive", "n": 92 },
        { "label": "compiled", "n": 78 },
        { "label": "small",    "n": 64 } ] } },
    View [ x = 28, y = 26, datapath = { parent.facts.value },
        layout: SimpleLayout [ axis = y, spacing = 16 ],
        Bar [ width = 300, datapath = :rows[], label = :label, value = :n ],
        ],

DataSource.url.name: url
DataSource.url.doc:
The resource URL — a literal or a `{ }` constraint, so the source **re-points reactively**:
change a dependency (a zip, a filter) and the *next* `fetch()` hits the new URL. Setting
`url` never triggers a load on its own — fetching stays explicit.

docSegs.md:
The resource URL — a literal or a `{ }` constraint, so the source **re-points reactively**:
change a dependency (a zip, a filter) and the *next* `fetch()` hits the new URL. Setting
`url` never triggers a load on its own — fetching stays explicit.

DataSource.format.name: format
DataSource.format.doc:
What the fetched bytes **are**: `"json"` (the default) parses the response and tags it for
`:path` navigation; `"text"` delivers the raw bytes as **one string** in `.value`. Text is
a first-class material, not a detail — it is how an authored Markdown file is loaded
*directly*, with no JSON wrapping beside it: consume it whole, `text = { article.value || "" }`.
A string has nothing to navigate into, so the structural machinery — `datapath`, `:path`,
`schema`, `<->` — does not apply to a text source; the lifecycle (`fetch()`, `.loading`,
`.loaded`, `.failed`) is identical. Loaded text renders clean through `Markdown`: HTML
comments in the file are annotation, and annotation never renders.

```declare-fragment
article: DataSource [ url = "guide.md", format = "text" ],
doc: Markdown [ visible = { article.loaded }, text = { article.value || "" } ],
```

docSegs.md:
What the fetched bytes **are**: `"json"` (the default) parses the response and tags it for
`:path` navigation; `"text"` delivers the raw bytes as **one string** in `.value`. Text is
a first-class material, not a detail — it is how an authored Markdown file is loaded
*directly*, with no JSON wrapping beside it: consume it whole, `text = { article.value || "" }`.
A string has nothing to navigate into, so the structural machinery — `datapath`, `:path`,
`schema`, `<->` — does not apply to a text source; the lifecycle (`fetch()`, `.loading`,
`.loaded`, `.failed`) is identical. Loaded text renders clean through `Markdown`: HTML
comments in the file are annotation, and annotation never renders.

```declare-fragment
article: DataSource [ url = "guide.md", format = "text" ],
doc: Markdown [ visible = { article.loaded }, text = { article.value || "" } ],
```

DataSource.method.fetch.name: fetch
DataSource.method.fetch.doc:
Loads (or reloads) the resource from the current `url`, then settles `value` and the status
flags **together**, a frame ahead. This is the whole point of DataSource: loading is a verb
you call — there is no auto-load — so you decide *when* (usually in `onInit`, or on a user
action). Calling it again re-fetches; change `url`'s dependencies first to fetch a new
address. Returns a `Promise` you can `await`, but the reactive flags are the idiomatic path.

docSegs.md:

flags **together**, a frame ahead. This is the whole point of DataSource: loading is a verb
you call — there is no auto-load — so you decide *when* (usually in `onInit`, or on a user
action). Calling it again re-fetches; change `url`'s dependencies first to fetch a new
address. Returns a `Promise` you can `await`, but the reactive flags are the idiomatic path.

DataSource.method.clear.name: clear
DataSource.method.clear.doc:
Returns the source to the idle state — drops `value`, `error`, and the loaded/failed flags,
as if it had never fetched. For resetting a search field's results, or releasing a large
response you no longer need.

docSegs.md:
Returns the source to the idle state — drops `value`, `error`, and the loaded/failed flags,
as if it had never fetched. For resetting a search field's results, or releasing a large
response you no longer need.

DataSource.name: DataSource
DataSource.doc:
A `Dataset` whose value arrives over HTTP — a reactive remote resource. The one thing to
know: **`fetch()` is explicit — a DataSource does not auto-load.** Call it when the data
should load (typically `onInit`, or on a user action); value and status then settle
*together*, a frame ahead, so a constraint reading `.loaded` and one reading `.value`
never disagree. Read the lifecycle through bindings — `.loading`, `.loaded`, `.failed`,
`.value`, `.error` — none are settable attributes; `clear()` returns it to idle. The
response is parsed JSON by default; `format = "text"` fetches textual material instead —
a Markdown article, a source file — as one string (see `format`).

```declare
weather: DataSource [ url = { `/api/weather?zip=${zip}` } ],
onInit() { this.weather.fetch() },                       // nothing loads until you ask
report: View [ visible = { weather.loaded },
    datapath = { weather.value?.rss?.channel } ]         // :paths below read the response
```

docSegs.md:
A `Dataset` whose value arrives over HTTP — a reactive remote resource. The one thing to
know: **`fetch()` is explicit — a DataSource does not auto-load.** Call it when the data
should load (typically `onInit`, or on a user action); value and status then settle
*together*, a frame ahead, so a constraint reading `.loaded` and one reading `.value`
never disagree. Read the lifecycle through bindings — `.loading`, `.loaded`, `.failed`,
`.value`, `.error` — none are settable attributes; `clear()` returns it to idle. The
response is parsed JSON by default; `format = "text"` fetches textual material instead —
a Markdown article, a source file — as one string (see `format`).

```declare
weather: DataSource [ url = { `/api/weather?zip=${zip}` } ],
onInit() { this.weather.fetch() },                       // nothing loads until you ask
report: View [ visible = { weather.loaded },
    datapath = { weather.value?.rss?.channel } ]         // :paths below read the response
```

Animator.attribute.name: attribute
Animator.attribute.doc:
The target slot this animator drives — a **bare attribute name** of the enclosing view
(`attribute = opacity`), checked to be numeric. Not a string, not a path; the raw slot
reference.

docSegs.md:
The target slot this animator drives — a **bare attribute name** of the enclosing view
(`attribute = opacity`), checked to be numeric. Not a string, not a path; the raw slot
reference.
The value to animate **to**.

Animator.from.name: from
Animator.from.doc:
The value to start **from** — **omit it to sample the target's current value** at start, so
an animation begins wherever the view already is (the usual case).

docSegs.md:
The value to start **from** — **omit it to sample the target's current value** at start, so
an animation begins wherever the view already is (the usual case).
The run time, in ms.

Animator.repeat.name: repeat
Animator.repeat.doc: How many times to repeat; `0`/absent runs once.

docSegs.md: How many times to repeat; `0`/absent runs once.

Animator.motion.name: motion
Animator.motion.doc:
The easing curve — a motion token (`quad`, `back`, …) naming the shape of the interpolation
between `from` and `to`.

docSegs.md:
The easing curve — a motion token (`quad`, `back`, …) naming the shape of the interpolation
between `from` and `to`.

Animator.relative.name: relative
Animator.relative.doc:
When true, `to`/`from` are **offsets** from the current value rather than absolute targets —
"move by", not "move to".

docSegs.md:
When true, `to`/`from` are **offsets** from the current value rather than absolute targets —
"move by", not "move to".

Animator.started.name: started
Animator.started.doc:
Set true to run it — the declarative `start()`. Drive it from a constraint to gate playback
on state.

docSegs.md:
Set true to run it — the declarative `start()`. Drive it from a constraint to gate playback
on state.

Animator.paused.name: paused
Animator.paused.doc:
Freeze a running animation and resume it without resetting — distinct from `started`, which
runs it from the top.

docSegs.md:
Freeze a running animation and resume it without resetting — distinct from `started`, which
runs it from the top.

Animator.method.start.name: start
Animator.method.start.doc:
Runs the animation from the current attribute value toward `to` — the imperative trigger,
equivalent to setting `started = true`. Prefer binding `started` to a condition when the run
is state-driven; call `start()` for a one-shot fired from a handler (`onClick() { fx.start() }`).

docSegs.md:
Runs the animation from the current attribute value toward `to` — the imperative trigger,
equivalent to setting `started = true`. Prefer binding `started` to a condition when the run
is state-driven; call `start()` for a one-shot fired from a handler (`onClick() { fx.start() }`).

Animator.method.stop.name: stop
Animator.method.stop.doc:
Halts the run where it is and leaves the attribute at its current value — it does **not**
snap back to `from`. Pair with `start()` for handler-driven control, or drive `started`
reactively instead.

docSegs.md:
Halts the run where it is and leaves the attribute at its current value — it does **not**
snap back to `from`. Pair with `start()` for handler-driven control, or drive `started`
reactively instead.

Animator.event.start.name: start
Animator.event.start.doc: Fires when the run begins (answered by `onStart`).

docSegs.md: Fires when the run begins (answered by `onStart`).

Animator.event.stop.name: stop
Animator.event.stop.doc: Fires when the run ends (answered by `onStop`) — the hook for chaining or cleanup.

docSegs.md: Fires when the run ends (answered by `onStop`) — the hook for chaining or cleanup.

Animator.event.repeat.name: repeat
Animator.event.repeat.doc: Fires at each loop of a repeating run (answered by `onRepeat`).

docSegs.md: Fires at each loop of a repeating run (answered by `onRepeat`).

Animator.name: Animator
Animator.doc:
Drives one numeric attribute from `from` to `to` over `duration`, on a motion curve — the
**timed** half of the animation family. A non-visual node you place *inside* the view it
animates, naming the target slot with `attribute`. For motion toward a **live, reactive**
target (one that keeps moving), reach for `Spring` instead — an Animator runs a fixed
`from`→`to` and stops.

```declare
box: View [ opacity = 0,
    fadeIn: Animator [ attribute = opacity, to = 1, duration = 300, started = true ] ]
```

docSegs.md:
Drives one numeric attribute from `from` to `to` over `duration`, on a motion curve — the
**timed** half of the animation family. A non-visual node you place *inside* the view it
animates, naming the target slot with `attribute`. For motion toward a **live, reactive**
target (one that keeps moving), reach for `Spring` instead — an Animator runs a fixed
`from`→`to` and stops.

App [
    box: View [ opacity = 0,
        fadeIn: Animator [ attribute = opacity, to = 1, duration = 300, started = true ] ]
]

AnimatorGroup.attribute.name: attribute
AnimatorGroup.attribute.doc: A default target slot cascaded to any member that omits its own (see `Animator.attribute`).

docSegs.md: A default target slot cascaded to any member that omits its own (see `Animator.attribute`).

AnimatorGroup.to.name: to
AnimatorGroup.to.doc: The default destination, inherited by members that omit `to`.

docSegs.md: The default destination, inherited by members that omit `to`.

AnimatorGroup.from.name: from
AnimatorGroup.from.doc: The default start value, inherited by members that omit `from`.

docSegs.md: The default start value, inherited by members that omit `from`.

AnimatorGroup.duration.name: duration
AnimatorGroup.duration.doc:
The default run time (ms), inherited by members that omit `duration` — the usual way to
give a whole group one tempo.

docSegs.md:
The default run time (ms), inherited by members that omit `duration` — the usual way to

AnimatorGroup.repeat.name: repeat
AnimatorGroup.repeat.doc: The default repeat count for members that omit `repeat`.

AnimatorGroup.motion.name: motion
AnimatorGroup.motion.doc: The default easing curve for members that omit `motion`.

AnimatorGroup.process.name: process
AnimatorGroup.process.doc:
How the members run — `sequential` (each starts when the previous finishes) or
`simultaneous` (all together). The one control unique to a group.

docSegs.md:
How the members run — `sequential` (each starts when the previous finishes) or
`simultaneous` (all together). The one control unique to a group.

AnimatorGroup.relative.name: relative
AnimatorGroup.relative.doc: The default offset-vs-absolute mode for members that omit `relative`.

docSegs.md: The default offset-vs-absolute mode for members that omit `relative`.

AnimatorGroup.started.name: started
AnimatorGroup.started.doc: Set true to run the whole group.

docSegs.md: Set true to run the whole group.
Freeze/resume the group without resetting.

AnimatorGroup.method.start.name: start
AnimatorGroup.method.start.doc:
Runs the whole group (sequencing or paralleling its members per `process`) — the imperative
equivalent of `started = true`.

docSegs.md:
Runs the whole group (sequencing or paralleling its members per `process`) — the imperative
equivalent of `started = true`.

AnimatorGroup.method.stop.name: stop
AnimatorGroup.method.stop.doc:
Halts the group and all its members in place. Members keep their current values; nothing
rewinds.

docSegs.md:
Halts the group and all its members in place. Members keep their current values; nothing
rewinds.

AnimatorGroup.event.start.name: start
AnimatorGroup.event.start.doc: Fires once when the group begins — after its members are sequenced, not per member.

docSegs.md: Fires once when the group begins — after its members are sequenced, not per member.

AnimatorGroup.event.stop.name: stop
AnimatorGroup.event.stop.doc:
Fires once when the **last** member finishes, so it means the whole group is done — the
place to chain the next thing.

docSegs.md:
Fires once when the **last** member finishes, so it means the whole group is done — the
place to chain the next thing.

AnimatorGroup.event.repeat.name: repeat
AnimatorGroup.event.repeat.doc: Fires at each loop of a repeating group (the outer group's repeat, not a member's).

docSegs.md: Fires at each loop of a repeating group (the outer group's repeat, not a member's).

AnimatorGroup.name: AnimatorGroup
AnimatorGroup.doc:
Coordinates several `Animator`s as a unit, running them `sequential` (one after another,
the default) or `simultaneous` (all at once). Its own `to` / `from` / `duration` / `motion`
/ `attribute` / `relative` / `repeat` are a **default cascade** — a member that omits one
inherits the group's — so shared timing is written once, on the group.

```declare
reveal: AnimatorGroup [ process = sequential, duration = 200,
    Animator [ attribute = opacity, to = 1 ],
    Animator [ attribute = y,       to = 0 ] ]
```

docSegs.md:
Coordinates several `Animator`s as a unit, running them `sequential` (one after another,
the default) or `simultaneous` (all at once). Its own `to` / `from` / `duration` / `motion`
/ `attribute` / `relative` / `repeat` are a **default cascade** — a member that omits one
inherits the group's — so shared timing is written once, on the group.

App [
    reveal: AnimatorGroup [ process = sequential, duration = 200,
        Animator [ attribute = opacity, to = 1 ],
        Animator [ attribute = y,       to = 0 ] ]
]

Spring.stiffness.name: stiffness
Spring.stiffness.doc:
Spring stiffness — higher pulls to the target faster (and can overshoot). The "how eager"
knob.

docSegs.md:
Spring stiffness — higher pulls to the target faster (and can overshoot). The "how eager"
knob.

Spring.damping.name: damping
Spring.damping.doc:
Damping — higher settles with less bounce. Low damping + high stiffness gives a springy
overshoot; high damping gives a smooth glide with none.

docSegs.md:
Damping — higher settles with less bounce. Low damping + high stiffness gives a springy
overshoot; high damping gives a smooth glide with none.

Spring.mass.name: mass
Spring.mass.doc:
The moving mass — heavier trails more slowly, for a looser, longer follow (a lagging cursor
dot).

docSegs.md:
The moving mass — heavier trails more slowly, for a looser, longer follow (a lagging cursor
dot).

Spring.epsilon.name: epsilon
Spring.epsilon.doc:
The rest threshold: how close to `to` counts as settled, at which point the spring **sleeps**
(and stops costing anything). Larger = sleeps sooner, at the price of stopping a hair short.

docSegs.md:
The rest threshold: how close to `to` counts as settled, at which point the spring **sleeps**
(and stops costing anything). Larger = sleeps sooner, at the price of stopping a hair short.

Spring.name: Spring
Spring.doc:
The **follow** half of the animation family: drives its `attribute` toward a **live,
reactive `to`** with spring physics — re-settling whenever `to` changes, and **sleeping at
rest** (zero cost while idle). Use it wherever the target keeps moving: a cursor trail, a
header that springs in on scroll, a value that chases wherever data puts it. Contrast
`Animator`, which runs a fixed `from`→`to` and stops. It descends from `Animator`, so it
inherits `attribute` and `to`; its own knobs are the spring constants.

```declare
dot: View [ width = 10, height = 10, cornerRadius = 5, fill = turquoise,
    followX: Spring [ attribute = x, to = { app.pointerX - 5 },
                      stiffness = 120, damping = 14, mass = 0.6 ] ]
```

docSegs.md:
The **follow** half of the animation family: drives its `attribute` toward a **live,
reactive `to`** with spring physics — re-settling whenever `to` changes, and **sleeping at
rest** (zero cost while idle). Use it wherever the target keeps moving: a cursor trail, a
header that springs in on scroll, a value that chases wherever data puts it. Contrast
`Animator`, which runs a fixed `from`→`to` and stops. It descends from `Animator`, so it
inherits `attribute` and `to`; its own knobs are the spring constants.

App [
    dot: View [ width = 10, height = 10, cornerRadius = 5, fill = turquoise,
        followX: Spring [ attribute = x, to = { app.pointerX - 5 },
                          stiffness = 120, damping = 14, mass = 0.6 ] ]
]

example.name: Spring
example.source:
App [ on: boolean = false,
    onMouseDown() { on = !on },
    View [ x = 28, y = 26,
        layout: SimpleLayout [ axis = y, spacing = 24 ],
        Text [ textColor = slategray, text = "click anywhere to toggle" ],
        View [ height = 64, width = 64, cornerRadius = 32, fill = dodgerblue,
            Spring [ attribute = x, to = { on ? 240 : 0 },
                stiffness = 190, damping = 17 ],
            ],
        ],
    ]

State.applied.name: applied
State.applied.doc:
Whether the state is on. Bind it to a condition (`applied = { expanded }`) and the whole
bundle snaps in and out as that flips — the base values restore themselves when it turns
off. The declarative alternative to the `apply()` / `remove()` / `toggle()` verbs.

docSegs.md:
Whether the state is on. Bind it to a condition (`applied = { expanded }`) and the whole
bundle snaps in and out as that flips — the base values restore themselves when it turns
off. The declarative alternative to the `apply()` / `remove()` / `toggle()` verbs.

State.method.apply.name: apply
State.method.apply.doc:
Turns the bundle **on** imperatively — the verb form of `applied = true`. Prefer binding
`applied` to a condition; reach for `apply()` only when the toggle is truly event-driven and
has no natural boolean to bind.

docSegs.md:
Turns the bundle **on** imperatively — the verb form of `applied = true`. Prefer binding
`applied` to a condition; reach for `apply()` only when the toggle is truly event-driven and
has no natural boolean to bind.

State.method.remove.name: remove
State.method.remove.doc:
Turns the bundle **off**, restoring the base values via the precedence stack (no manual undo).
The partner of `apply()`.

docSegs.md:
Turns the bundle **off**, restoring the base values via the precedence stack (no manual undo).
The partner of `apply()`.

State.method.toggle.name: toggle
State.method.toggle.doc:
Flips the bundle on/off from its current state — a one-call switch for a handler
(`onClick() { panel.toggle() }`) when you don't want to thread a boolean.

docSegs.md:
Flips the bundle on/off from its current state — a one-call switch for a handler
(`onClick() { panel.toggle() }`) when you don't want to thread a boolean.

State.event.apply.name: apply
State.event.apply.doc:
Fires when the bundle turns **on** — after the overrides compose onto the stack, so reads
in the handler already see the state's values. Use for a side effect that must ride with the
state (focus a revealed field), not to mutate what the state already declares.

docSegs.md:
Fires when the bundle turns **on** — after the overrides compose onto the stack, so reads
in the handler already see the state's values. Use for a side effect that must ride with the
state (focus a revealed field), not to mutate what the state already declares.

State.event.remove.name: remove
State.event.remove.doc:
Fires when the bundle turns **off**, the partner of `onApply` — after the base values have
restored. For tearing down whatever `onApply` set up.

docSegs.md:
Fires when the bundle turns **off**, the partner of `onApply` — after the base values have
restored. For tearing down whatever `onApply` set up.

State.name: State
State.doc:
A named bundle of overrides that snaps on and off **together**. Its body is not ordinary
children: a `name = value` entry is an **override of the enclosing view's own attribute**
(checked against *that* view's schema, not the State's), applied while the state is on;
an `id: Type [ … ]` entry is a conditional subtree that exists only while applied. Because
attribute reads compose as a **precedence stack**, the base values return *exactly* when
the state turns off — there is no manual undo to write, and no drift.

```declare
card: View [ height = 72, fill = white,
    open: State [ applied = { expanded }, height = 184, fill = lightsteelblue,
        Text [ text = "shown only while open" ] ],
]
```

(checked against *that* view's schema, not the State's), applied while the state is on;
an `id: Type [ … ]` entry is a conditional subtree that exists only while applied. Because


example.name: State
example.source:
App [ fill = white, textColor = black,
    open: boolean = false,
    onMouseDown() { open = !open },
    card: View [ x = 28, y = 26, width = 300, height = 72,
        cornerRadius = 10, fill = whitesmoke,
        Text [ x = 16, y = 16, fontWeight = bold, text = "Summary" ],
        big: State [ applied = { open }, height = 184, fill = lightsteelblue,
            Text [ x = 16, y = 54, width = 268, wrap = true,
                text = "height, colour, and this whole line swap in together" ],
            ],
        ],
    ]

Node.name: Node
Node.doc:
The plain object-graph atom — a **non-visual** node you subclass for logic that isn't a
view: a controller, a coordinator, a service. A bare `class X [ … ]` **defaults its base to
`Node`**, so a class with attributes and methods but no box *is* a Node subclass. It lives in
the tree as a named member, shares the reactive core and the `classroot`/`app` reach, and
fires `init` — but it paints nothing. Reach for it instead of a View when a wrapper class
would be visual in name only.

```declare
class Cart [ count: number = 0,
    add()    { count = count + 1 },
    clear()  { count = 0 } ]
```

A view then holds one as a named member (`cart: Cart [ ]`) and reads/drives it reactively —
state and behaviour with no pixels of its own.
